Overview

The Business Problem

With the federal REAL ID Act enforcement deadline approaching in October 2020, North Carolina's DMV was facing a major surge in demand. Millions of residents needed to convert their driver's licenses — but the existing 114 physical locations and 27 mobile units weren't enough to handle the volume, especially in rural and underserved areas.

The NC Department of Transportation needed a data-driven answer to a high-stakes question: Where should new DMV locations be opened to maximize coverage and minimize wait times across the state? Our team at Fayetteville State University partnered with NCDOT to solve it.

Methodology

How We Solved It

We applied a rigorous, multi-stage analytical framework combining GIS spatial analysis, supply-demand modeling, and multi-criteria decision analysis.

01
Capacity Gap Analysis
Modeled supply vs. demand at each existing DMV using county census data (15+ population) and workstation capacity. Identified 29 high-demand locations with 40+ unserved customers per day.
02
GIS Suitability Mapping
Used ArcGIS to build Euclidean distance rasters for 6 key location factors: highway exits, shopping malls, universities, hospitals, population density, and proximity to existing sites.
03
AHP Weighting
Applied Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) with pairwise comparisons across 16 decision criteria to objectively weight each factor based on expert input — removing bias from the ranking process.
04
Weighted Sum Analysis
Normalized all raster layers to a 0–100 scale and combined them using weighted sums in ArcGIS, generating a statewide suitability map highlighting the most viable candidate zones.
05
15-Mile Proximity Filter
Applied 15-mile radius proximity analysis over the suitability map, overlaying existing location coverage gaps to pinpoint 18 specific candidate locations for further evaluation.
06
Factor Rating & Final Ranking
Used MCDA Factor Rating Method to score all 18 candidate locations across 16 weighted criteria, producing a final prioritized ranking to guide NCDOT's capital investment decisions.
Presentation

Results

Key Findings

📍
29 Under-Capacity Sites
Identified 29 existing DMV locations (21 physical, 8 mobile) unable to handle the projected REAL ID surge — each with 40+ unserved customers per day.
🗺️
18 Viable New Locations
GIS suitability analysis across NC identified 18 candidate locations in coverage gaps, prioritizing both urban and rural accessibility within a 15-mile service radius.
🏆
Bahama, NC Ranked #1
After multi-criteria evaluation across 16 weighted factors, Bahama (Durham County) emerged as the highest-priority site, followed by Gibsonville and Black Mountain.
⚖️
Objective Decision Framework
AHP pairwise comparisons eliminated subjective bias in the site selection process — a replicable model NCDOT can apply to future facility location decisions.
Final Rankings

Top Recommended Locations

All 18 candidate locations were ranked using the Factor Rating Method. Lower rank = higher priority for new DMV placement.

Rank Location County
1BahamaDurham
2GibsonvilleGuilford
3Black MountainBuncombe
4FarmvillePitt
5MoncureChatham
6ClimaxRandolph/Guilford
7Four OaksJohnston
8MiddlesexNash
9LocustStanly
10Jackson SpringsMoore
